title: When You Can't Forgive
subtitle: A reading on what forgiveness actually is, what it is not, and when it isn't available yet
excerpt: A reading on the common misconceptions about forgiveness, what the research actually describes it as, and what to do when the capacity for it is not yet there.
sourceNotes: Draws on published forgiveness research including Robert Enright's process model and Everett Worthington's REACH model of forgiveness. Framing is original to homemade.education.

practice.whenToUse: Use when you feel stuck on the question of forgiveness: whether you should, whether you can, and what it would actually mean.

content.text: Most people who struggle to forgive are working with a definition of forgiveness that makes it impossible. They believe forgiveness means condoning what happened, or forgetting it, or reconciling with the person who caused harm. Under that definition, refusing to forgive is the only defensible response to a real wrong. The difficulty is that the definition is wrong.

Forgiveness is not a statement about the other person's actions. It does not say what happened was acceptable. It does not require contact with the person. It does not require that they apologise first, or that you tell them you have forgiven them. It is not a single event that, once achieved, stays achieved. It is not something you owe to the person who harmed you.

Forgiveness research describes it as a shift in the person doing the forgiving: a reduction in the hostility, resentment, and desire for revenge that follows a genuine wrong. That shift is for the benefit of the person forgiving, not the person forgiven. The research on its effects is consistent: people who move toward forgiveness report lower anxiety and better sleep. The benefit accrues internally, independent of anything the other person does.

Robert Enright's process model describes forgiveness as a staged process, moving from uncovering the anger through a deepening phase. Everett Worthington's REACH model maps a similar five-step process from recall through to holding the forgiveness. Both frameworks take for granted that the process takes time and is not linear.
When the capacity is not there yet

Forgiveness cannot be willed into existence ahead of the process that makes it possible. If the wound is recent, if the anger has not been fully named yet, if the grief has not been worked through, the capacity for forgiveness is not yet available. That is not failure. It is sequence.

What is available before forgiveness is possible is this: you can choose not to act on the resentment. You can set it down temporarily rather than carry it through every interaction. You can acknowledge that you want, eventually, to be free of it, without requiring yourself to achieve that freedom on a schedule.
Draws on Robert Enright's process model of forgiveness and Everett Worthington's REACH model, both published in peer-reviewed psychology literature. Framing is original to homemade.education.
